theorem_title: Lean Eval: Oppenheim inequality for Hadamard products
theorem_statement: Let A and B be real square positive semidefinite matrices indexed by a finite type n. Let A o B denote their entrywise, or Hadamard, product. Then det(A) times the product of the diagonal entries of B is at most det(A o B).
raw_proof_text:
We first reduce to the case where A and B are positive definite and the diagonal entries of B are all equal to 1. If A and B are only positive semidefinite, replace them by A_epsilon = A + epsilon I and B_epsilon = B + epsilon I. These matrices are positive definite for epsilon > 0, and the desired semidefinite inequality follows by taking epsilon -> 0 because determinants and entries vary continuously.

Assume now that A and B are positive definite. Since B is positive definite, every diagonal entry B_ii is positive. Let D be the diagonal matrix with entries B_ii^{-1/2}, and put C = D B D. Then C is positive definite and C_ii = 1 for every i. Also B_ij = B_ii^{1/2} C_ij B_jj^{1/2}. Therefore A o B = D^{-1} (A o C) D^{-1}. Taking determinants gives det(A o B) = (prod_i B_ii) det(A o C). It remains to prove that if A is positive definite and C is positive definite with diagonal entries 1, then det(A o C) >= det(A).

We prove this normalized statement by induction on the matrix size. The case n = 1 is immediate because C_11 = 1, so A o C = A. For the induction step, write A in block form as [[a, x*], [x, A1]] and C as [[1, y*], [y, C1]], where A1 and C1 are positive definite and C1 has diagonal entries 1. Since A is positive definite, its Schur complement a - x* A1^{-1} x is positive. The Hadamard product A o C has block form [[a, x* o y*], [x o y, A1 o C1]]. By induction, det(A1 o C1) >= det(A1).

It remains to compare Schur complements. We claim that (x o y)* (A1 o C1)^{-1} (x o y) <= x* A1^{-1} x. To prove this, observe that the block matrix M = [[x* A1^{-1} x, x*], [x, A1]] is positive semidefinite, because its Schur complement with respect to A1 is zero. The matrix C is positive semidefinite, and the Schur product theorem says that the entrywise product of positive semidefinite matrices is positive semidefinite. Thus M o C is positive semidefinite. Written in block form, M o C is [[x* A1^{-1} x, x* o y*], [x o y, A1 o C1]]. Positivity of this block matrix implies its Schur complement is nonnegative, giving the desired inequality.

Finally, det(A o C) = det(A1 o C1) [a - (x o y)* (A1 o C1)^{-1} (x o y)]. Using the induction hypothesis and the Schur complement comparison gives det(A o C) >= det(A1) [a - x* A1^{-1} x] = det(A). This completes the induction and the proof.

Likely Formal Islands graph: normalize B to diagonal 1; use the Schur product theorem; prove the Schur complement comparison; apply the determinant induction step; pass from positive definite to semidefinite by perturbation. The best islands are the Schur complement comparison and the positive-definite induction step.
